With that said, have at it!

comments.title: Child's perception of people and color Part 2
comments.content:
I'm the mom from last week, whose 2 year old girl associates people with colors. There's been a development I want to share, as well as an exchange that leads to me seek more advice.
Last week my daughter and I were talking: she was calmly trying to get around a consequence she didnt like (assigned by my husband), and I was not budging on it for the sake of presenting a unified front, while internally feeling conflicted because I would have handled it very differently and because I was secretly pleased with her attempts to problem solve. Our conversation was quite relaxed, playful even, despite my inner conflict.
Suddenly, my daughter's expression morphed into a delighted sort of astonishment and she sharply drew in a breath. "Mama, your face TURNED ORANGE!"
The surprise in her face & voice was unmistakably authentic. Also noteworthy is that she called me "mama" which she's mostly grown past in favor of "mommy." She now only uses "mama" in vulnerable moments, like when she wakes up at night wanting me.
She let her hand hover near my cheekbone while her eyes were fixed on my face. She repeated "Your face is orange!"
That moment was so beautiful. I adore observing her in times of excitement or wonder. This was definitely one of those times. I was absorbed in watching her expression, and feeling truly awed that she was able to see in this unique way, when her eyes lit up once more and she delightedly exclaimed "Mommy your face turned PINK!"
Dad, who had witnessed this whole thing (and who later confirmed I was not blushing), came up to us and asked her, "What color is MY face?"
She turned towards him, then poked his cheek, "Your face is purple." (Poor dad is the same old purple he's always been.)
The next incident has led me to do a lot of thinking. My daughter enjoys "quizzing" me for fun. She'll ask me a series of questions to which she already knows the answers (mainly naming body parts, or identifying people or animals by their pictures). Yesterday for the first time she quizzed me on color. "What color is daddy?"
I know HER answer for this, and I hesitated on whether to say what she expects to hear, or to be honest about our differing experiences. Finally I said: "I dont know what color daddy is. I need YOU to tell me. You can see people's colors. Mommy cannot see people's colors."
Her eyebrows ticked upwards when I said that last sentence, and she spent about 10 seconds just pondering in silence. At long last, she simply said, "Daddy is purple," and moved on to the next (not color based) question in her quiz.
I confess that I cant help finding this all to be weird. Beautiful, yes, but, still very odd. And while I dont want to lie and pretend that I see things I don't see, I also dont want to make my daughter to feel negatively about it.
My questions
1. Am I correct in assuming my emotions are likely to effect the way my aura appears to her? (Both my orange and pink face was preceded by strong emotion on my part)or is it more likely to be something else? (You mentioned last week that she's likely seeing the "primary" color of people's auras, which suggests we always have more than one color. Perhaps shes just noticing these other colors during close conversation?)
2. If my emotions DO affect my aura, is there a way to not be so transparent to her? It seems distinctly unfair to burden a child with an adult's emotions. (My father is terminally ill, for instance, and that comes with lots of feelings.) I know to some extent children are always sensitive to emotions, but I'd rather not have a glowing face signal if I can help it.
2. Are there resources (preferably online) that you'd recommend I use to educate myself about auras? Things I'm finding with a Google search seem dubious. For example, your answer last week that different people perceive auras in different ways seems much more realistic than the pat responses I found, which seem geared toward making people feel good about themselves. (Eg, Blue aura people are SO wise and spiritual and amazing!) I'm seeking resources that are informational (something that goes into what IS an aura, how do people perceive/experience them etc) rather than instructional (I'm not looking at training myself to see them). My goal is to lessen my ignorance so that I can be less weirded out by it, and can talk to my daughter from an informed position if she has questions.
3. If she still has these perceptions when she is older, I will likely encourage her to keep them private. My motive is to avoid exposing her to stigma/disbelief/mockery. In your view, is that advisable? If so, any pointers on avoiding making it a matter of shame?

replies.title: Re: Child's perception of people and color Part 2
replies.content:
1) What's probably happening is that your daughter's psychism is starting to pick up emotional patterns as well as the basic tone of the aura. That's a normal process, though it does suggest that she's got considerable clairvoyant capacities.

2) Nope. She's going to see what she sees. Since all she sees is the colors, though, it'll be a while before she starts figuring out which feeling is indicated by which color.

3) There are plenty of resources, fortunately. If you're up for a serious dose of occult philosophy, Arthur Powell's The Astral Body is a good source if somewhat outdated in some of its language and attitudes -- you can download it for free here. Besant and Leadbeater's book Thought-Forms (available for download here) may also be worth your while.

4) That's not merely advisable, it's essential. Most families that have hereditary clairvoyance (this is quite common in people of Celtic descent) make this an ordinary part of life; there are things you don't talk about outside the family, because other people don't understand. (My wife was raised with that rule, for example.) Make it clear that it's not shameful, it's just something private. Explaining to your daughter that she sees the colors and other people don't, as you've already done, is a very good step in that direction.

replies.title: Re: Child's perception of people and color Part 2
replies.content:
Thank you so much! I sincerely appreciate your insight.
As it turns out, my husband is a mix of Celtic & Nordic descent, if you look back far enough. His family has lived in the US for generations, though, and aren't connected to their roots at all. He, at least, is unaware of any hereditary clairvoyance in his family.
But now that I think of it, he is uncannily aware of my emotions, even back in the earliest days of our marriage. Despite my having a mild disposition and reasonable self-restraint, I am utterly unable to prevent him from noticing if I'm upset or troubled.
Wow, this has been fascinating!

replies.title: Re: Child's perception of people and color Part 2
replies.content:
I'd like to second our host's advice in the strongest possible terms: teach your daughter not to talk outside of family about any unusual abilities or perceptions, and teach her what is usual, what is not.

It frightens most adults, sometimes very badly, when a child has unusual abilities, or--even more so--when a child can effortlessly see beneath the surface of the everyday masks that adults wear in public, or can see the outcome of events too clearly. I blundered badly that way a few times when I was still a child, and it was sheer luck that the results weren't disasterous for me.

You can also teach her to "read" the very subtle cues that happen when she is starting to frighten some adult, and teach her how to defuse the fear by pretending it was just a mistake or a lucky bit of guesswork.

A badly frightened adult is capable of doing literally anything in response.

replies.title: Re: Child's perception of people and color Part 2
replies.content: Thank you; I will take your warning to heart. I hadn't considered fallout more severe than getting her feelings hurt, and maybe having people pressure me to put her in counseling.

replies.title: Re: Child's perception of people and color Part 2
replies.content:
I have no useful advice here, but it makes me happy that you are putting in the effort to understand what's going on with her, tell her the truth, and not "correct" her. :)

I grew up with a host of audio/visual anomalies which I felt unable to talk to anyone about. It was not healthy.

replies.title: Re: Child's perception of people and color Part 2
replies.content:
I'm with you more than you know on that. God was always very real and present to me, especially as a child. Finding out that this was a taboo topic... was pretty heartbreaking. I mean, if you can't take God seriously at church, where the heck *can* you? Still a practicing Christian. Not a practicing occultist. Finally found a church where actual belief isn't embarrassing, and where encounters with saints and things are considered a pretty normal part of the human experience. Not just OK, but really no big deal.

I find it hilarious that this little pagan corner of the internet is such a deeply comfortable place: some folks here are personally uncomfortable with God, but they're not dismissive. I don't know how JMG pulls that off (well, ok, good moderating), but it is a truly lovely thing and I deeply appreciate it.

On the original topic: Most of my audio/visual weirdnesses are probably neurological- they are common to many migraineurs, dyslexics, synaesthetes, and people on the autistic spectrum. I don't read much into them. But any time I brought it up as a kid, my parents squelched it-- they didn't want to hear it, and they wanted to make darn sure I didn't tell anyone outside the family, for fear of folks thinking I was nuts. When a new visual phenomenon cropped up in my early twenties (dark shadow-movement hallucinations in my peripheral vision), I didn't tell a soul. It turned out to be a symptom of an autoimmune thyroid disorder that nearly killed me, and maybe I could have gotten a proper diagnosis years earlier if I'd just told a neurologist or endocrinologist about it. Sigh.

It seems important to be honest, keep communication open within the family, and teach basic rules about what's appropriate to talk about and with whom... but not to ever make such things either a matter of shame, or of "this makes you more special than everybody else". Or at least, that seems like a practical path, and I wish my parents had taken it.

replies.title: Re: Child's perception of people and color Part 2
replies.content: Methylethyl (if I may), thank you! How I make it work -- well, other than tight moderating, it's very simple: I believe in gods. As in, all of them. For a polytheist, that's not at all difficult, and it allows me to relate to (and thus to model, for other people, relating to) people of many different religions in a way that accepts what they have to say about their gods.

replies.title: Re: Child's perception of people and color Part 2
replies.content:
You're welcome!

What the scriptures say, and what the church has believed from the beginning, is far more congruent with the assumptions and worldview found here, than with mainstream American denominations and secular society at large (those are very similar). A world where the supernatural is just as real as the physical world, and where it affects and interacts with it, seems to be the default human assumption the world over. Modern secularism is the anomaly.

The most fundamental difference is not in our perceptions of reality, but what we're permitted to do about it. Our goal is to get to an unreserved "Thy will be done" with our particular God. You, not being devoted to that particular God, assume a certain leeway to say "my will be done". All of which is a far cry from "If you can't document it scientifically, it doesn't exist."

comments.title: Astro influences & transcendence
comments.content:
JMG,

I'm curious as to what you think of this: I've heard some contend that it's possible to spiritually transcend all astrological influences, completely so. I can readily believe that most people really are unconsciously subject to the push and pull of astro forces - a negative aspect between Mars and Pluto, e.g., and a lotta people walk around stewing without really knowing why. Of course the more self-aware one becomes (and more astrologically savvy perhaps), the more emotionally even-keeled one is during a time of stressful astro influences.

This is different, of course, than transcending astro influences all together. I imagine that no matter how spiritually evolved one is, one never escapes the influences of the natal chart and the impact that the ever-changing astro configurations have on the chart. I'm thinking that the astro impact becomes much more spiritually subtle and defined, perhaps relating to the "higher biosphere" of the earth's etheric and astral levels on a more clearly defined basis. In any event, I don't imagine that we can escape the earth's bio/etheric/astral-spheres, nor should we want to - we can, however, change the "tone" of astro influence to one of more subtle, spiritual impact.

Thank you,
Will M

replies.title: Re: Astro influences & transcendence
replies.content: As far as I know, while you have a physical body, you're going to be influenced by the astrological forces that imprinted that body at birth, and while you exist on this planet, you're going to be influenced by the broader context of astrological energies. You can certainly learn to work consciously with those forces, rather than being pushed around by them willy-nilly, but you're always part of the whole system of the cosmos.

replies.title: Re: Astro influences & transcendence
replies.content:
I can't locate the precise post, but Gordon White of Rune Soup wrote a piece with some extremely interesting speculations about the function of the Great Pyramid of Giza, namely that it was built as a massive ritual chamber rather than a tomb. He argues that it was a locus for an initiation rite undergone by Pharaohs and that one of the intended effects was to allow the participants to be liberated from the constraints of their natal chart by being symbolically inseminated into the womb of the sky goddess Nut through the phallus of the earth God Geb (represented by the pyramid) and then be "reborn" by descending back to Earth under stellar conditions of their choosing. He suggests that this process of rebirth by ascent to the heavens and then descent to earth is what's referred to in an Egyptian ritual text as "coming down under another sky."


Of course, whether it worked as intended is an open question. And even if it did the fact that the Egyptians felt it necessary to construct a massive stone pyramid to carry it off suggests that this kind of transformation is beyond what can be achieved by an ordinary occultist nowadays!

replies.title: Re: Astro influences & transcendence
replies.content: We have no idea if it worked or not, and if it did, the result seems to be simply that you get a different natal chart -- not that you're freed from celestial influences altogether. It seems much more sensible to me to learn how to work with the forces you've got -- after all, that's the chart that defines what you need to do to grow spiritually in this life...

comments.title: The 22 Paths
comments.content:
Greetings Mr. Greer! Thank you for hosting Magic Monday. It's interesting to see what questions come up every week.

My question is, is there any specific reason why the Hebrew letters are assigned alphabetically from top to bottom in the Hermetic Tree of Life? The oldest published version of the ToL diagram has less than 22 paths but later versions have 22 paths with 3 of them horizontal, 7 are vertical, and 12 are diagonal. The numbers are very specific so I believe this is not a coincidence.

(neetwizard)

replies.title: Re: The 22 Paths
replies.content:
There are many different ways to assign the Hebrew alphabet to the Tree of Life, and there's good reason for that. Dion Fortune points out in The Mystical Qabalah that the Sephiroth are objective but the Paths are subjective; what that means in practice is that the Sephiroth are basically the same for everybody, but how you travel from one to the other is much more open to variation.

If I recall correctly, it's Rabbi Isaac Luria's version of the Tree that makes use of the system you've hinted at here, with the mother letters assigned to the horizontal paths, the double letters to the vertical paths, and the single letters to the diagonal paths. Does that work? Sure. The top-down version is that of the Hermetic Order of the Golden Dawn. Does that work? Yep. Charles Stansfield Jones worked out a system that puts the letters onto the Tree from the bottom up, and that works, too. Then you have entirely different systems such as William Gray's English Cabala, which assigns the English consonants to the Tree, and you know what? That also works.

So if you'd rather assign the Hebrew letters and their associated symbolism to the Tree as you've suggested, by all means do so; there's every reason to think that'll work well. Please, though, if you can avoid it, don't fall into the trap of insisting that your way is the One True Way. It's a little like the legendary argument between the Englishman, the Frenchman, and the German as to whether the four-footed animal barking at them is a dog, un chien, or ein Hund...

replies.title: Re: The 22 Paths
replies.content:
I've never heard about the bottom-up system before! Thank you very much for the info. The first idea I thought about that was, "if the order of the alphabet is a metaphor for spiritual ascent, it makes sense to place the letters that way." So now I can see a way to justify the top-down system as well.

Yes, Rabbi Isaac Luria developed his sytem under the same assumption of matching the mothers with horizontals, etc. Later rabbis also follow this idea but they have different opinions about where exactly to place each letter of each set. Is it Alef-Mem-Shin or Shin-Alef-Mem? Is the order of the diagonals right-left-right-left, right-left-left-right, or what?

And as you pointed out, somehow they just work.

(neetwizard)

replies.title: Re: The 22 Paths
replies.content: Jason Miller's works dealing with the classical planets discuss a similar idea with respect to the assignment of Greek vowel tones to the different planets. He notes that some works assign them in ascending order and others in descending, and suggests that magicians could alternate depending on whether a given spell is aimed at transcendence or manifestation.

comments.content:
This just came up, coming into focus only in the last hour, and now it's got me on edge: Both my wife and l have lost a series of items in the last month. While I was typing my questions above, she had been scouring the house looking for some silk she planned to sew with, and is beside herself that she can't find it. She also can't find her musical chord chart, several other important books and other items. I have lost 3 or 4 calculators, an item I previously lost once every 10 years if that. I also have a backpack which disappeared in the last several weeks, it contained books only, including LRM, The Grail Quest, and Jung's Man and his Symbols.

There is a context here that may or may not be pertinent, but it is what sets me on edge: My wife is very concerned about a crime that happened to us after she led a public memorial service at our local city hall last fall for victims of the Pittsburg synagog shooting. This event was fairly well publicized in advance, and there were several hundred people in attendance. The day after the service, I was driving with my wife and 2 daughters on a shopping errand, and as we got off the freeway, I found I had no brakes. Through some uncanny good luck, lack of traffic and green lights, I was able to get the car safely to a stop. The mechanic who worked on our car said the brakes were sabotaged. Police found a puddle of brake fluid where I park in front of our house. Given that we are Jewish, and the publicity and my wife's Facebook page imply and/or state she is Jewish, we think this is an anti-Jewish hate-crime, attempted murder no less. No suspects have been identified, and the investigation is cold. So we have installed surveillance cameras around our house, my wife has taken a self-defense class. I now have a revolver, and need to get my backside in gear for firearms training, and am looking at getting a concealed carry permit (The local Freemasons lodge has an interest group for shooters, I plan on connecting with them if I get admitted). Anyway, my wife and I were just now comparing notes about all these things vanishing, then it struck her that this could somehow be connected to that crime. I said "No way. Why would they steal a chord chart, silk fabric, calculators, a backpack with books, and ignore a $2000 guitar, and an even more valuable silver flute and harp in plain sight?"

So I'm just now trying to put all this together as I write. These disappearances themselves are small potatoes, but they do seem disturbing if for no other reason that they suggest covert break-ins. Rekeyed locks are in order at minimum.

I certainly have fodder for divination. But I am a neophyte, and need as much good input as I can get. Any thoughts?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: lunarapprentice, it occurs to me that the randomness of these thefts make sense if the perpetrator is trying to harass rather than make a profit. Maybe they don't care what they steal? Maybe they're just trying to shake you and your wife up. I hope you get this sorted out quickly. Good luck and God bless.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Thank you. It's complicated, and it really It doesn't help that, when I wrote that submission, that I was sick with a fever, taking pseudoephrine, which has a known side-effect of anxiety, and my wife and I basically experienced a trigger of what must be PTSD. Our synagogue's security contractor thinks the facts of the case point to a lone perpetrator rather than an organized group, and he was clearly taking precautions not to get caught, and he opined that a repeat attack seems unlikely. We need to assimilate that whoever did this almost certainly never get caught.

Since I started to deal with magic I had my slow tricking share of weird stuff happening. The book was on aliens; maybe it was the good people praying their pranks. Or not; who knows?

comments.title: Long hair and turban
comments.content:
Hello JMG,

What are the occult reasons, if any, to let one’s hair (and beard) grow long?
We know Samson lost all his strength when Delilah had his hair cut.
Also, we see that in Islam having a beard is almost compulsory.

On a side note, one Kundalini Yoga practitioner told me they recommend using a turban as a means of maintaining the head cool during and after practice , otherwise the subject will be prone to headaches and mental confusion. I couldn’t get confirmation of this anywhere, though.

Could you comment on these topics?

Thank you.

replies.title: Re: Long hair and turban
replies.content: There are religions and spiritual paths in which it's important to shave the top of your head, and religions and spiritual paths in which it's important to wear the hair long and cover the top of your head. My guess is that it relates to different ways of activating the pineal gland, which is sensitive to light. In Chinese religion, for example, Taoists traditionally grew their hair long and wore little caps to cover the crown of the head, while Buddhists shaved their heads. More research here is needed -- but it seems, as a very general overview, that people shave their heads when they're trying to transcend the natural world, while they grow their hair long when they're trying to live in balance with the natural world.

replies.title: Re: Long hair and turban
replies.content:
This is fascinating! I'm Eastern Orthodox, and we have a long tradition of female head-covering (and long-haired monks, and prayer caps...). But because of modern lifestyles and feminism, it's a radioactive topic to bring up. It's not a mandatory practice, and when you ask about it, you get the same three or four unsatisfactory answers. I figured it was a carryover from Judaism, but then Judaism seems to have spiritual reasons for it. When I converted, this really bothered me. After several years, I decided I needed to get to the bottom of it, and that perhaps, like many religious things, it was not amenable to being logically *explained* but rather had to be *experienced*.

So, on that theory, I just *did* it. Started wearing a scarf or bandana as daytime wear, not just at church. I can't explain exactly, but I felt different (wish I could be more specific). It was still perplexing to me, and six months or a year in, I was at an evening service devoted to the Mother of God, and I was talking to her. Not about head covering, just generally-- I'm from a Protestant background, and Protestants have issues with Mary. I was trying to work those out. I mulled over all the things she actually says in the Gospels: "My soul magnifies the Lord" etc.

And then, unexpectedly, she answered my question. It was like she was gently saying "here, look." and I saw an unusually geometric version of the platytera icon (the one where Mary is holding the the Christ child in a big circle on her lap-- but with lots more concentric circles), and a woman facing it, bowing, and tying her scarf behind her head, reverently. If I were more archetype-minded, I'd say it had something to do with the divine feminine. But I'm not a theologian or a philosopher, and I can't really say what that meant, except that it answered the specific question of "should I or shouldn't I?" to my own satisfaction. When asked, I stick with "it's devotional" but I reckon that's oversimplifying it.

Dunno if that's a useful data point at all, but... covering the head: there's something to it. Darned if I know exactly what. Fascinating to contemplate why other religions might do it.

replies.title: Re: Long hair and turban
replies.content: Hi Methylethyl!  I too am a Christian and I wore a scarf for years and years; I wasn't raised Christian and no-one tried to make me wear a scarf, I did it because it made sense to me.  After about 18 years, it became suffocating, and I don't routinely wear a scarf for devotional reasons anymore. I haven't sorted that all out yet...to be continued.  Cheers  --Sidney

comments.title: How to let go of "people pleasing" mode.
comments.content:
Because of my fear of being considered a "bad" person, or having even an indirect causal realtionship to someone else's pain, I've been very much prone to the whole slew of emotional manipulation tactics that exist that keep me in a chronic people pleasing mode.

I do try to challenge myself with honestly expressing what I think and feel sometimes in the contexts (outside of frank talks I can have with my love partner) but my mind still ends up obsessing and chastising me if I get a negative response, trying to figure out why or what I did wrong.

I want so much the freedom to be hated, disliked, and to maintain my emotional and intellectual bearing even in the face of disapproval and dedicated efforts to subtlely conform me to their sense of ethic or power game.

What can I do to keep myself more clear of these herd-molding mentalities I still have within me? How can I still listen deeply, keep and open mind, but without sacrificing my own position and security in the face of these cheap tricks of social submission that I'd spent so many years under?

Why do I value a stranger's feelings about things more than I do my own? What can I do about it?

replies.title: Re: How to let go of "people pleasing" mode.
replies.content:
It really does depend. One of the reasons that those red MAGA hats and the ideology that goes with them have become so popular of late is that a lot of people are embracing the freedom that you want. That's a little like learning how to swim by jumping in the deep end of the pool, but it's an option -- there's a serious rush that comes from saying the unthinkable, watching people hate you and knowing that you really, truly don't have to care.

But you have to want that more than you want to "listen deeply" and "keep an open mind" -- those are useful servants but bad masters, and they too often turn into just another part of the power game. There's nothing gained by listening deeply to someone who's speaking shallowly, you know, and minds are like ovens -- if you leave them open all the time, everything comes out half baked.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Sure -- that was a good long time ago.

The first magical working I ever did that gave me the TSW reaction was simple enough; I did a Lesser Banishing Ritual of the Pentagram, and felt the shift in the energies of the space. It was nothing spectacular, but I could feel it as clearly as I'd feel, say, a sudden drop in temperature. I sat down on the ugly little naugahyde-covered couch we had back then, and just stared at nothing for a good long while, processing it.

The most impressive magical working I've done that's far enough back that I can talk freely about it -- that was my first serious Golden Dawn talisman. I left college in 1983 after three years, six majors, and no clue what I wanted to do with my life. In 1990 I decided it was time to go back and finish my degree. The difficulties were that (a) my wife and I were dirt poor, (b) she was still paying off student loans from her degree, and (c) that had been enough of a difficulty for us that I wasn't willing to borrow a penny of money for college.

So I did a classic GD talisman of Jupiter during the planetary day and hour. It was a good solid ritual, and I followed it up by applying to the university I wanted to attend and filling out the financial aid application the next day. And...

...the state government promptly passed a bill providing a mess of financial aid for men over the age of 29 who were heads of households and were returning to college after time in the workforce. Between a couple of grants and a work-study position, I had the money for my first year, and my grades were high enough that I got an academic scholarship to fill things out for the second year. I graduated in 1993 magna cum laude without one penny of student loan debt. TSW...

comments.title: Magic or coincidence
comments.content:
I am trying to figure out if something I am dealing with has a magical component and what to do if it does. I would appreciate your thoughts.

I joined an church with a lot of Amish input in my late teens. That family of churches (Amish, Mennonite, and relatives) work on promises, and people tend to keep those promises. I left in my early twenties--more than 20 years ago--and was excommunicated. Excommunication is, fundamentally, a public, formal statement that this person has not kept his promises, backed up by the community's promise to avoid any voluntary interaction with this person until he keeps his promises.

Many times, over the years since, I've felt that people just don't take promises to me seriously. And over the past several months, meditating on it, I've noticed that other people who left have the same experience. And very frequently, it's in ways that make it very difficult to keep their own promises: people promise to pay them and don't, making it very difficult to pay the people they owe (but not impossible--just enough to make it a huge sacrifice to do so). Spouses are unreasonably difficult to get along with (what I'm dealing with personally), right up to the edge of infidelity, without being openly unfaithful. And so on and so forth.

I've started to wonder if this has a magical component. It makes sense that someone who breaks formal, public promises would find it harder the next time, but it seems like the promises being very difficult to keep is really common.

I'm Catholic, but the church (I've talked to my priest) doesn't really have a category for "not demonic, not Catholic, but real magic." And since I'm still unwilling to keep the promises I made as a teen, I can't ask the church I left to lift the ban and stop considering me a vow-breaker.

If there's a magical component, how would I know and what would I want to do differently?

Thank you,
SamChevre

replies.title: Re: Magic or coincidence
replies.content:
If it were me, I would skip the churches entirely and speak directly to the God to whom you made the promises. Explain what happened and why, ask what reparations must be made for the broken vows (and then wait for an answer, ask forgiveness (especially since asking forgiveness figures so prominently in Christianity) genuinely repent, and then do somthing as an act of penance that you find appropriate. At this point, perhaps make a new vow, if that seems appropriate and if you are certain it is within your power to keep the new vow (it does not have to be the same vow or even a remotely similar one). The whole process might take months of meditation and prayer, or the whole thing could come together in an evening... it depends on how familiar you are with human-to-God interactive communication (which does take practice but is SOOO FULFILLING once mastered) and how much you have already thought about all this beforehand.

Regardless of whether or not there is a magical component to your circumstances, this will strengthen your relationship with your God.

Bear in mind that people of Western society have lost a great deal of their moral compass, particularly with the loss of community. The broken promises might actually be the normal amount of broken promises everyone else is dealing with, it may just catch your attention more since you were once a part of a community that held vows sacred. Nevertheless, you can work to live up to your own standard of honor regardless of the society in which you live now, if you choose.

Sincerely,
Jessi Thompson
anotheramethyst

replies.title: Re: Magic or coincidence
replies.content:
Yes, there's a magical component. You've been cursed by the church -- that's a standard part of excommunication, and it's something the Catholic church also does in its excommunication ceremonies.

Fortunately traditional Catholic lore offers a straightforward response. You should invoke the Blessed Virgin and ask her for her help. She's the one to whom Catholics have been turning since the Middle Ages to help them get out of spiritual bondage of various kinds. Obviously the rosary is a powerful tool here, but don't neglect other aspects of Marian devotion. You need her help; ask for it, and do the necessary spiritual disciplines to put yourself in an appropriate condition to receive it.

replies.title: Re: Magic or coincidence
replies.content:
Thank you! I pray the rosary semi-regularly, and my priest has recommended the Novena of Mary Undoer of Knots for other tangled struggles in the past, so I will pray that for this problem.

And yes--Amish/Mennonite ritual work is very heavily Catholic (with the key difference that it is much more communal), and looking at this situation with that fact in mind, it helps explain why they are so scrupulously careful to keep promises: the raspberry jam principle makes the consequences of not doing so more than usually bad.

SamChevre

Looking at your past question and this one, the impression I get is a sensation I'm a little too familiar with in different areas of life: would I be right in guessing that the will to move forwards is there but you have nothing to thrust on and so it feels like you're trying to gain traction on air?

1) Finding your tamanous strikes me as a metaphor that distorts more than it reveals. My impression is that you don't have to find your tamanous at all - to paraphrase a certain television show of my youth*, "it has always been there", and the trick is in perceiving it. (It occurs as I type this that perhaps the original point of the vision quest was simply to exhaust the initiate enough that he could see what was always there but had never noticed - a possibility I hadn't thought of before, so thanken for a seed of inspiration!)
2) Your comment about wanting to find the things that seem good/true/beautiful to you sounds very similar to how I see the world; moreover, looking at your comment a couple of weeks back, your described history with the Faustian and Magian worldviews sounds similar to my own experiences with the Faustian worldview and whichever deep culture is backing the Southern and/or evangelical divine order hierarchy (I think either the Magian or Mesoamerica, but I'm not sure). That strikes me as promising, assuming the direction you're trying to head is similar to the one I'm aiming for; in particular, I sometimes wonder if the experience of rejecting both sides of a binary and taking a ternary position is important to the North American experience.
3) My instinct was and remains that the old ways of interacting with North America left with (the dieoff of) the native** tribes and we have to build new ones of our own.

** - I think "native American" might actually be a shining example of the Amerind sense of humor; I'm not sure anyone can truly be native to North America, even if they're from a tribe that's lived on the continent for ten thousand years.

comments.title: Magic education
comments.content:
JMG-

First off, thank you so much for doing these magic mondays, I've learned more from this than any other online source.

Anyway, I have a follow up question on a topic I suspect you're tired of, but I'm going to ask anyway.

My children are 6 (twin girls) and 4 (boy). I'm well aware of all the various things I should and shouldn't do around them, my question is about magical education for them.

And I mean that in the broadest sense, not rituals, but philosophy, concepts and ways of thinking that are, shall we say, less common in current culture.

I should probably say we home school.

I figure my kids are a bit young for anything right now. I don't want to force anything down their throats either. I have told them about the idea of reincarnation, but beyond that I haven't said. But I'd like to start thinking about sort of basic magical curriculum for the future and maybe see if anyone else has thought these things through already -- like at what age is a good age to introduce, say, the idea that numbers can have meaning beyond quantity, or when one might be old enough to make sense of Tarot symbolism and so on (like I said, not rituals, that's something I leave for them to discover on their own if they have that inclination).

so my question is, are you aware of anyone who's written anything about magical education, particularly with regard to magical philosophy/worldview? I thought of R Steiner, since he's an occultist and was interested in education, but frankly his ideas on education seem a bit crazy to me, particularly regarding karma and children, but maybe I've misunderstood him. Beyond that I haven't found much. I know you've talked about natural magic as a good starting point for teaching kids, but as of right now I know absolutely nothing about natural magic.

replies.title: Re: Magic education
replies.content: My daughter really enjoyed the Herb Fairies series by Learning Herbs for a kid-friendly start with natural magic.  You can download their first book for free to check it out - https://learningherbs.com/herb-fairies/

replies.title: Re: Magic education
replies.content:
Steiner's ideas were a bit crazy, though I know people who've sent their children to Waldorf schools and the kids came out very well indeed. A bit of craziness is not necessarily a drawback. I don't know of anyone else who's written on occult schooling.

Since I haven't had kids to raise in this life, I'm not the best person to ask. The one thing I'd very strongly encourage is to raise them on old myths, legends, and folk tales, not the ghastly plastic crap that passes for kiddie lit these days. Old children's books, sure -- you can go very far with the Newbery Award books -- but make sure their imaginations are fed with myth. I sometimes think that half the reason I ended up able to think my way out of the wet paper bag of contemporary culture is that I got handed the D'Aulaire's Norse Gods and Giants at an impressionable age, and went gaga for every kind of myth thereafter.

replies.title: Re: Magic education
replies.content:
The 'Aulaires' Norse Gods and Giants was the first book that I, too, ever was given as a young child which offered me anything like mythology (or spirituality or religion), and I eagerly devoured it. (I already knew that my father's grandparents were all born in Denmark, which just made the book all that more personally significant for me.)

Looking backward, I think I mostly educated myself, once I had learned to read. Teachers and lessons were a very small part of my education; books that I read in solitude did most of the heavy lifting. When we had children of our own, the one thing that seemed to me to be most important was to teach them to read before they ever entered Kindergarten.

That turned out not to be all that hard to do. We used the small oblong Peanuts paperbacks as first readers. Our children already loved having us read the funnies to them, and Peanuts was in every newspaper back then. Like me and my wife, our children both turned out to have insatiable curiosity, which we encouraged in every way we could.

replies.title: Re: Magic education
replies.content: My mother supplied us with a whole collection of library-discard books of mythology and fairy tales. The Andrew Lang books were enchanting, but I think you lose something by jumping in and out of different cultural traditions. What I want for my own kids now: a really good book of Russian folklore and fairy tales. Of the lot, those were the most captivating to me. That Hansel and Gretel witch never bothered my thoughts after the book closed, but Baba Yaga sure did!

comments.title: Understanding Amissio and Acquisitio in context
comments.content:
Hello again Mr. Greer and thank you for your Magic Mondays.

Another pair of geomancy-related question for you, kind sir, this time related to the variable perception of Acquisitio and Amissio.

I apologize for the length of the setup of each question, but hopefully their roots will be clear and straightforward to answer.

1. If one asks something such as “should I sell X”, how does one factor Amissio and Acquisitio - does wanting to sell something count as a “loss” such that their normal significations become reversed?

  For instance, for this question, I got Amissio as the right witness, Via as the Left Witness, and Acquisitio as the Judge.

  I interpreted this as “you will lose (the item) to gain (money) which you can use to travel (perhaps metaphorically, to open doors for other things)” or perhaps “you might have to sell it at a loss but even so the end result is gain (compared to not selling it)”? Or am I all wrong and since I want to ‘lose’ the item, the judge is somehow negative in this question?

2. With regards to relocation-based questions (“should I move” / “should I stay”) and derivatives (“will I be happy if I move/stay?”), is the fourth house interpreted inversely depending on if the question is move vs. stay, and how does denial/perfection change the answer given by the figures?

  Concretely, I asked “will I be happy if I stay in this apartment another year?” and got Carcer in the first and Amissio in the fourth house* with a strongly perfected chart (conjunction in the fifth), and a day later asked “should I move?” and got Conjunctio in the first and Amissio in the fourth house** with a denied chart (impedition).

  I took the first chart to say “No you won’t be happy if you stay” (carcer and amissio are both negative and the perfection emphasizes it?) and the second chart to say “Moving is strongly unadvised” (negative Amissio, denied = strong negative?). But if I look at carcer as emphasizing staying put, perhaps the first chart is saying “NOT moving is strongly advised” and the second is also saying “no, don’t move”?

  Are either of these the correct way to interpret Amissio versus the question, especially with respect to denial or perfection?   

And how would I weigh Amissio/Acquisitio in the judge/witnesses in these two situations, too, as for/against moving or yes/no to the question directly (and which is yes, which is no, is moving a loss? is staying a gain?). 

* * Mothers: Carcer, Albus, Tristitia, Amissio, LW Conjunctio, RW Populus, Judge Conjunctio, Reconciler Fortuna Major
* ** Mothers: Conjunctio, Puer, Albus, Amissio, LW Conjunctio, RW Albus, Judge Acquisitio, Reconciler Fortuna Minor

replies.title: Re: Understanding Amissio and Acquisitio in context
replies.content: Ahem. That's why it's important to cast a daily chart, interpret it, and then check your interpretation against what happens. You'll learn what these figures mean for you. As for perfection and denial, that depends on whether there's a mode of perfection linking the significators, not on your interpretation of the significator!

Hi JMG,

I think that my workplace is haunted. My lines of evidence are--

Weird experiences, including visual and tactile phenomena, experienced by three different people. One saw a translucent, shadowy snake writhing on the floor; she was frightened and commented that she "Never sees things like that." The second felt hands grabbing at her legs. The third felt something cold and unpleasant touch her when the place was mentioned in a different setting.

Difficult, intrusive thoughts experienced by a number of people. In my own case, it was regular unhappy thoughts about my romantic partner, who also works there. Eventually I decided to stop following the thoughts, and simply see where the feeling was manifesting in my body. I felt intense tightness and discomfort in my throat and heart regions. I also experienced flashes of unpleasant imagery in my imagination. Based on conversations with my partner and observations of my coworkers, I believe this is fairly common. It seems that half the place is going through a breakup or divorce.

A geomancy reading. The question was "Is there actually a dark spirit in [the workplace.]" Puella in the 8th house seemed to be an affirmative. The significator of the querent was Puer. Puer springs, in the chart, to the fifth house, to conjunct Puella in the fourth house. This suggested to me that the answer is Yes, and that the entity in question is related to sexual energies (the dynamic between Puer and Puella, and the involvement of the 5th house) and to the physical structure of the place (it is located underground, and it is constructed of substances that are used to absorb etheric energies in magic-- I can't go into more detail without revealing too much).

Given the evidence, do you think I'm correct in my interpretation? And if so, what would you do? I semi-regularly have the chance to perform the LBRP and Rose Cross rituals when I'm there, but not in every room, and, while it does objectively appear to help, it doesn't seem to resolve the problem. I'd be grateful for any advice you might have.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: Might be an entity, might be someone with unresolved sexual tensions -- that's often the cause of poltergeist activity. This sort of thing is best handled by specialists. If you aren't one, and can't get one, your best bet is to concentrate on keeping yourself energetically clean with regular banishings and Rose Cross rituals.
